concepts.title: Introduction
concepts.conceptBody:
Basics of rotational motion

Consider a disk rotating on a stationary rod.

If we now view this disk from the top,

we see that when the disk rotates so that the arc length (s) equals the length of the radius of the disk $\left( r \right)$, the subtended central angle $\left( \theta \right)$ will equal 1 radian. The resulting equation is

$\begin{matrix} \text{s}=\text{r}\theta \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \theta =\text{s}/\text{r} \\ \end{matrix}$

where the unit of a radian represents the dimensionless measure of the ratio of the circle's arc length to its radius.

If the disk rotates through one complete revolution, then $\mathbf{s}$ equals the entire circumference and $\theta $ equals $2\pi $ radians.

$\begin{matrix} \text{s}=\text{r}\theta \\ 2\pi \text{r}=\text{r}\theta \\ \theta =2\pi \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ radians }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{matrix}$

Differentiating the basic equation $s=r\theta $ results in the next two relationships between the tangential motion of a point along the circumference and the angular behavior of the disk itself.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{s} & ~=\text{r}\theta \\ \text{ds}/\text{dt} & ~=\text{r}\left( \text{d}\theta /\text{dt} \right) \\ \text{v} & ~=\text{r}\omega \\ \end{array}$

The Greek letter $\omega $ represents the angular velocity of the disk. It is measured in the unit radians/sec . Differentiating one more time, we have

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{v} & ~=\text{r}\omega \\ \text{dv}/\text{dt} & ~=\text{r}\left( \text{d}\omega /\text{dt} \right) \\ \text{a} & ~=\text{r}\alpha \\ \end{array}$

The Greek letter $\alpha $ represents the angular acceleration of the disk. It is measured in the unit $\text{radian}/\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}$ . 

Summary 

These three equations

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{s}=\text{r}\theta \\ {} & \text{v}=\text{r}\omega \\ {} & \text{a}=\text{r}\alpha \\ \end{array}$

allow us to relate the linear motion $\left( \text{s},\text{v},\text{a} \right)$ of a point moving in circular motion on a rotating platform with the rotational motion $\left( \theta ,\omega ,\alpha \right)$ of the platform itself.

It is important when using these equations that the units on $\text{s},\text{v}$, a and $\text{r}$ be consistent. That is, if the radius is measured in meters, then s must also be in meters, $\text{v}$ in $\text{m}/\text{sec}$, and a in $\text{m}/\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}$. Using our new variables for angular motion, we can now state analogous equation to those we have already used for linear motion. When the velocity is constant our equations are

$\text{s}=\text{vt }\!\!~\!\!\text{ which }\!\!~\!\!\text{ becomes }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta =\omega \text{t}$

The following chart shows the relationship between the equations for uniform linear acceleration and those that deal with uniform angular acceleration. When working with pure rotational motion, the standard unit of measurement is the radian.

concepts.title: Angular Velocity
concepts.conceptBody:
Angular Velocity $\left( \omega \right)$ 

{Caution : Do not confuse with angular frequency}

Angular velocity describes the speed of rotation and the orientation of the instantaneous axis about which the rotation occurs. The direction of the angular velocity vector will be along the axis of rotation; in this case (counter-clockwise rotation) the vector points towards the viewer.

It is defined as "the rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time". 

In physics, the angular velocity is a vector quantity (more precisely, a pseudo vector) which specifies the angular speed, and axis about which an object is rotating. The SI unit of angular velocity is radians per second, although it may be measured in other units such as degrees per second, revolutions per second, degrees per hour, etc. When measured in cycles or rotations per unit time (e.g. revolutions per minute), it is often called the rotational velocity. Angular velocity is usually represented by the symbol omega ( $\omega )$. The direction of the angular velocity vector is perpendicular to the plane of rotation, in a direction which is usually specified by the Right hand grip rule.

Two dimensions 

The angular velocity of the particle at $\text{P}$ with respect to the origin $\text{O}$ is determined by the perpendicular component of the velocity vector $\text{v}$.

The angular velocity of a particle in a 2-dimensional plane is the easiest to understand. As shown in the figure on the right (typically expressing the angular measures $\varphi $ and $\theta $ in radians), if we draw a line from the origin $\left( \text{O} \right)$ to the particle $\left( \text{P} \right)$, then the velocity vector $\left( \text{v} \right)$ of the particle will have a component along the radius (radial component, ${{\text{v}}_{\parallel }}$) and a component perpendicular to the radius (tangential component, ${{\text{v}}_{\bot }}$).

A radial motion produces no rotation of the particle (relative to the origin), so for purpose of finding the angular velocity the parallel (radial) component can be ignored. Therefore, the rotation is completely produced by the tangential motion (like that of a particle moving along a circumference), and the angular velocity is completely determined by the perpendicular (tangential) component.

It can be see that the rate of change of the angular position of the particle is related to the tangential velocity by :

${{\text{v}}_{\bot }}=\text{r}\frac{\text{d}\phi }{\text{dt}}$

Utilizing $\theta $, the angle between vectors ${{\text{v}}_{\bot}}$and $\text{v}$, or equivalently as the angle between vectors $\text{r}$ and $\text{v}$, gives :

${{\text{v}}_{\bot }}=\left| \text{v} \right|\text{sin}\left( \theta \right).$

Combining the above two equations and defining the angular velocity as $\omega =\text{d}\varphi /\text{dt}$ yields :

$\omega =\frac{\left| \text{v} \right|\text{sin}\left( \theta \right)}{\left| \text{r} \right|}$

In vector form the linear velocity, angular velocity and radius vector are related by

$\vec{v}=\vec{\omega }\times \vec{r}$

Thus, angular velocity can be represented as a vector quantity whose direction is perpendicular to the plane of motion.

Points to Remember 

  1. If a particle is moving in a circle it is in pure rotational motion about the centre of the circle, while for a moment it may be in pure translational motion about some other point.
  2. If a particle $\text{P}$ is moving in a circle, its angular velocity about centre of the circle $\left( {{\omega }_{\text{c}}} \right)$ is two times the angular velocity about any point on the circumference of the circle $\left( {{\omega }_{0}} \right)$

or ${{\text{w}}_{\text{c}}}=2{{\omega }_{0}}$

This is because \(\angle \mathrm{P}^{\prime} \mathrm{CP}=2 \angle \mathrm{P}^{\prime}\) (by property of a circle)
\(\omega_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\angle \mathrm{P}^{\prime} \mathrm{CP}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{pp}^{\prime}}}, \omega_0=\frac{\angle \mathrm{P}^{\prime} \mathrm{OP}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{pp}^{\prime}}}\)

From these relations we can see that ${{\omega }_{c}}=2{{\omega }_{0}}$.

  1. If a rigid body is rotating about a fixed axis with angular speed $\omega $, all the particles in rigid body rotate same angle in same interval of time, i.e., their angular speed is same $\left( \omega \right)$. They rotate in different circles of different radii. The planes of these circles are perpendicular to the rotational axis. Linear speeds of different particles are different. Linear speed of a particle situated at a distance $r$ from the rotational axis is

or

$\begin{align} & \text{v}=\text{r }\omega \\ & \text{v}\propto \text{r} \\ \end{align}$

Angular velocity of a rigid body $\left( \omega \right)$ is $\frac{\text{d}\theta }{\text{dt}}$. Here $\theta $ is the angle between the line joining any two points (say A and B) on the rigid body and any reference line (dotted) as shown in figure. For example OP is a rod of length $5\text{ m}$. End $\text{O}$ is resting against a vertical wall $OY$ and $P$ is moving towards right with constant speed ${{V}_{P}}=10\text{ m}/\text{s}$. To find the angular speed of rod at $\theta =30$, we can proceed as under.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{AP}=\text{x}=\text{OP cos }\theta \\ \therefore & \text{x}=5\text{ cos }\theta \\ \text{ or } & \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=-5\text{ sin}\theta \left( \frac{\text{d}\theta }{\text{dt}} \right) \\ \therefore & \left( \frac{\text{d}\theta }{\text{dt}} \right)=-\frac{\left( \text{dx}/\text{dt} \right)}{5\text{sin}\theta }\left( \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}={{\text{V}}_{\text{P}}}=10\text{ m}/\text{s} \right) \\ \text{ or } & \omega =-\frac{10}{5\text{sin}{{30}^{\circ }}}=-4\text{rad}/\text{s} \\ \end{array}$

Here negative sign implies that $\theta $ decreases as t increases $\left( \frac{d\theta }{dt}<0 \right)$

concepts.title: Angular acceleration
concepts.conceptBody:
Angular Momentum 

A mass moving in a straight line has linear momentum $\left( {\vec{P}} \right)$. When a mass rotates about some point/axis, there is momentum associated with rotational motion called the angular momentum $\left( {\vec{L}} \right)$. Just as net external force is required to change the linear momentum of an object a net external torque is angular momentum is classified in following three types.

concepts.title: Kinetic energy of a rotating body
concepts.conceptBody:
Kinetic energy of a rotating body

Let us consider a rigid body performing pure rotation with angular velocity $\omega $ about the axis passing through the point O

$\begin{array}{l} L=\sum \text{m}\left( \omega {{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}} \right){{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}} \\ =\sum \text{m}\omega \text{r}_{\text{i}}^{2} \\ =\omega \sum \text{mr}_{\text{i}}^{2}=\omega \text{I} \\ \therefore \text{ L=I}\omega & {} \\ \end{array}$

Note : This is applicable about only that axis about which the body is under pure rotating motion.

$\begin{array}{l} \begin{align} & \Delta\text{ K}\text{.E }=\frac{1}{2}\Delta\text{ m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}\text{.} \\ & \text{K }=\sum \frac{1}{2}\text{ m}{{\left( \omega {{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}} \right)}^{2}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}{{\omega }^{2}}\sum \text{mr}_{\text{i}}^{2}=\left( \frac{1}{2}\text{I}{{\omega }^{2}} \right) \\ & \therefore \text{K }=\frac{1}{2}\text{I}{{\omega }^{2}} \\ \end{align} \end{array}$

Note : Applicable when the body is under pure rotatory motion in that frame about axis of rotation.

About any general axis

$\text{K}=\text{K'}+\frac{1}{2}\text{Mv}_{\text{CM}}^{2}$

$\text{K}=\frac{1}{2}{{\text{I}}_{\text{CM}}}{{\omega }^{2}}+\frac{1}{2}\text{Mv}_{\text{CM}}^{2}\text{ in }\text{ general }\text{ applicable }\text{ of }\text{ rigid }\text{ body }\text{ motion}\text{. }$

Now, since

$\begin{align} & \begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{L}=\text{I}\omega \\ {} &  \\ \end{array} \\ & \therefore \frac{\text{dL}}{\text{dt}}=\text{I}\left( \frac{\text{d}\omega }{\text{dt}} \right)+\omega \left( \frac{\text{dI}}{\text{dt}} \right) \\ & \\ \end{align}$

If $\text{I}$ is constant, then

$\tau =\frac{\text{d}L}{\text{dt}}=\left( \text{I}\alpha \right)\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ valid }\text{ only }\text{ about }\text{ that }\text{ axis } \\ \text{ about }\text{ which }\text{ the }\text{ body }\text{ is } \\ \text{ under }\text{ pure }\text{ rotatory }\text{ motion } \\ \end{array} \right\}$

(Here, $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration of the body about the same axis where I is calculated}

However, ${{\vec{\tau }}_{\text{ex}}}=\frac{\text{d}\vec{L}}{\text{dt}}$ is valid everywhere

Caution : Consider and check whether the axis is inertial or non-inertial.

In non-inertial axis, apply PSEUDO FORCE.

concepts.title: Angular acceleration of a rigid body
concepts.conceptBody:
Torque and Angular Acceleration for a rigid body 

The angular acceleration of a rigid body is directly proportional to the sum of the torque components along the axis of rotation. The proportionality constant is the inverse of the moment of inertia about that axis, or,

$\alpha =\frac{\sum ~\tau }{\text{I}}$

Thus, for a rigid body we have the rotational analog of Newton's second law :

Following two points are important regarding the above equation.

(i) The above equation is valid for rigid bodies. If the body is not rigid like a rotating tank of water, the angular acceleration $\alpha $ is different for different particles.

(ii) The sum $\Sigma \tau $ in the above equation includes only the torques of the external forces, because all the internal torques add to zero.

Rotation with constant angular acceleration 

Acceleration : 

We know that

$\text{v}=\vec{\omega }\times \vec{r}$

Taking the time derivative, $\vec{a}=\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}\left( \vec{\omega }\times {{{\vec{r}}}_{c}} \right)=\frac{d\vec{\omega }}{dt}\times \vec{r}+\vec{\omega }\times \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$

Here, $\frac{d\vec{\omega }}{dt}=\vec{\alpha }$. and $\frac{d \vec{r} P}{\text{dt}}=\vec{v}=\vec{\omega }\times \vec{r}$

Hence

By definition of the cross product. The first term on the right has a magnitude ${{a}_{t}}={{\alpha }_{r}}$, called the tangential component of acceleration. Likewise the second term has a magnitude ${{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}={{\omega }^{2}}\text{rsin}\theta ={{\omega }^{2}}\text{r}$ and called the normal component of acceleration.

The tangential component of acceleration represents the time rate of change in the velocity's magnitude and can also be written as $\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dt}}$. Thus, 
 

If the speed of $\text{P}$ is increasing, then ${{\vec{a}}_{\text{t}}}$ acts in the same direction as $\vec{v}$, if the speed is decreasing, ${{\vec{a}}_{t}}$ acts in the opposite direction of $\vec{v}$ finally if the speed is constant ${{\vec{a}}_{t}}$ is zero.

The direction of ${{\vec{a}}_{n}}$ is always towards O, the centre of the circular path. Hence, Eq. (vi) can be identified by its two components as $\vec{a}={{\vec{a}}_{t}}+{{\vec{a}}_{n}}=\vec{\alpha }\times \vec{r}-{{\omega }^{2}}\vec{r}$

Since, ${{\vec{a}}_{t}}$ and ${{\vec{a}}_{n}}$ are perpendicular, if needed the magnitude of acceleration can be determined from the Pythagorus theorem, namely $a=\sqrt{\text{a}_{\text{n}}^{2}+\text{a}_{\text{t}}^{2}}$

examples.title: A person moved from $A$ to $B$ on a circular path as shown in figure. If the distance travelled by him is $60 \mathrm{~m}$, then the magnitude of displacement would be: $\left(\right.$ Given $\left.\cos 135^{\circ}=-0.7\right)$

If the maximum speed with which the stone can revolve is $K / \pi r e v . / m i n$. The value of $K$ is :
(Assume the string is massless and unstretchable)

The radius of path taken by the person is $200 \mathrm{~m}$ and he takes $3 \mathrm{~min} 58 \mathrm{sec}$ to complete his journey. The average speed of the person is $\qquad$ $\mathrm{ms}^{-1} \cdot($ take $\pi=3.14)$

The ratio of the angular speed of minutes hand and hour hand of a watch is:
A wheel is at rest. Its angular velocity increases uniformly and become 80 radian second after 5 seconds. The total angular displacement is:
A body is revolving with a constant speed along a circle. If its direction of motion is reversed but the speed remains the same. Then, which of the following statement is incorrect?

Two bodies of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ moving in concentric orbits of radii $R$ and $r$ such that their periods are the same. Then the ratio between their centripetal acceleration is

Two racing cars of masses $\mathrm{m}_1$ and $\mathrm{m}_2$ are moving in circles of radii $\mathrm{r}_1$ and $\mathrm{r}_2$ respectively. Their speeds are such that each makes a complete circle in the same duration of time $t$. The ratio of the angular speed of the first to the second car is

A wheel is subjected to uniform angular acceleration about its axis. Initially its angular velocity is zero. In the first 2 seconds, it rotates through an angle $\theta_1$. In the next 2 seconds, it rotates through an additional angle $\theta_2$. Find the ratio of $\theta_2 / \theta_1$.

A stone tied to the end of a string of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ long is whirled in a horizontal circle with a constant speed. If the stone makes 22 revolution in 44 seconds, what would be the magnitude and direction of acceleration of the stone?
